data.title: Nvidia's China Revenue Constraints Don't Negate The Bull Case
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA), the stock has traded laterally.

Nvidia stock is likely facing more intense pressure on its China revenues following increased political friction that's likely following the 2025 "Victory Day" parade, where President Xi Jinping was seen directly alongside Russian President Vladimir Putin and North Korean leader Kim Jong Un. Given that the overarching message from the day was antithesis to "Western hegemony," there is a rational conclusion to be made that China's isolated nature on the global stage will likely increase following its outright detraction of the current geopolitical status quo.

Nvidia's Relationship With China

For years, China represented a double-digit share of Nvidia's revenue. In FY25, China and Hong Kong accounted for ~13% of total sales, but by Q2 FY26 that figure had fallen to ~6%, underscoring a rapid contraction. Singapore often masks U.S. demand, with >99% of Singapore-billed controlled compute sales actually headed to U.S. customers in recent quarters, not to China, which means that Nvidia's China revenue statements are clear and direct—we can essentially take them at face value.

At the moment, shipments of high-end AI accelerators to China are license-dependent, and many data center GPUs made by Nvidia fall within those thresholds. In August 2025, some H20 licenses were granted for sale to China, but Nvidia has not shipped or booked these, primarily because China is focusing on developing its own chips. China, by all indications, wants "independence," or isolation.

Recent optics will almost certainly tighten the policy bias. The "Victory Day" parade with Xi flanked by Putin and Kim Jong Un does not change the law, but it will almost certainly strengthen the hawkish frame in Washington, raising the odds of narrower, slower, or rescinded licenses.

When U.S. controls bite, Beijing backs domestic substitutes—a strategy it is leaning on more and more recently. The main vectors for this are Huawei Ascend (CANN/ModelArts stack), Biren BR100 (Hot Chips), and Moore Threads MTT S3000/MUSA. It's worth looking a little deeper into these companies for reflection on Nvidia's relative position to China's rising infrastructure.

Huawei was founded by Ren Zhengfei, who established the company in 1987 and remains its CEO. It's a privately held company, and its Ascend AI chips compete with Nvidia's GPUs, while its CANN + ModelArts is a software ecosystem designed to replace Nvidia's CUDA. Huawei is essentially the closest domestic stand-in for Nvidia in China.

Biren Technology has a goal to be China's high-end alternative to Nvidia in data centers. It was founded by Zhang Wen (ex-SenseTime) with veterans from Qualcomm (QCOM), Nvidia, and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). The company has built the BR100 GPU (an Nvidia H100-class competitor) on TSMC (TSM) 7nm with 77B transistors. The production is inevitably hit by U.S. export rules, so the ecosystem is still limited, but the technology is catching up.

Moore Threads is another rising Chinese semiconductor behemoth, founded in 2020 by Zhang Jianzhong, an ex-Nvidia VP. The team is stacked with ex-Nvidia engineers, including CTO Zhang Yubo. Its MTT S3000 and MUSA software stack is designed as an Nvidia CUDA ecosystem clone. This is perhaps one of the most stark examples of Chinese mimicry, and a potential direct long-term threat to America's geopolitical leadership. China appears to be directly vying for full industrial independence.

Given this environment, and broader, more complicated geopolitical competition between China and the West, we can see that regulatory pressures on China from the West will likely persist. To counter the U.S. export controls limiting Nvidia's ability to ship advanced GPUs to China, China's state funding is flowing directly to Huawei, Biren, and Moore Threads to reduce reliance on Nvidia. For Nvidia, China offers capped and lower-quality revenue that's license-dependent, and the West will inevitably remain the company's core growth engine. That said, just because China is focusing on its isolation, which it frames as "independence," that doesn't mean the rest of the world has to, or will, follow suit. Perhaps America needs greater independence from China, but America does not need independence from the rest of the world. This is a very positive elementary difference between the West and China and bodes much stronger for Nvidia than its Chinese counterparts. Nvidia has access to an open international architecture of markets, while China will be more limited to self-serve.

Nvidia's Thriving Western-Oriented Bull Case

CEO Huang himself has envisioned the AI infrastructure boom remaining massive through 2030, with a forecast of $3–4 trillion total addressable market by the end of the decade. Nvidia currently commands over 80% of the GPU market for AI training and deployment, pushing the likelihood of over $1 trillion in Nvidia's annual data-center revenue as likely if it can capture 55–60% of the market as it becomes saturated with more competition and diversification.

The truth is that despite China containment, Nvidia's Western-oriented architecture is the dominant growth engine of the global AI economy. The United States and its allies, including Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOGL, GOOG), Meta (META), Oracle (ORCL), and European hyperscalers, remain the largest, fastest-growing, and most capital-intensive spenders on accelerated compute. Put plainly: the bulk of the $3–4 trillion AI infrastructure supercycle is flowing through Western-aligned capital markets, clouds, and enterprises—this is the very ecosystem that Nvidia dominates and China is constrained from accessing. Put even more bluntly: Chinese Nvidia substitutes have their growth horizon capped predominantly to domestic use, while Nvidia can access the remaining economy of 7 billion people readily (this number excludes China's 1.4 billion population). This is not cold war, it is simply the reality of polycentrism and the nature of economically/ideologically allied vs. non-allied economies.

Valuation Update

Nvidia stock is currently positioned at a good entry point. The secular AI thesis is very much alive, and any fear about multiple compression is more than offset by structural medium-term growth remaining intact. In other words, moderation in returns does not mean contraction in returns, and likely future alpha is still present.

The stock's 14-day RSI is now 40, indicating moderately good value, especially when positioned alongside the price trading along the 50-day moving average.

We may be looking at Nvidia's forward P/E as likely trending down toward 25 over the next five years (the ratio is currently about 38). Even from the following chart, the stock should probably be trading around its average forward P/E right now, given the growth moderation we're facing. So, maybe NVDA is a little overvalued, but that doesn't really bother me (the growth is too good).

So, what are we dealing with in terms of a return estimate? If normalized EPS reaches $9.50 in January 2030, approximately four years away, and the forward P/E is 25 in January 2029, we're looking at a stock price of about $240 in three years. The current price is $170, so that's only about a 12% three-year CAGR. Not bad, but not great.

But let's backtrack a bit. What about in two years? If January 2029 normalized EPS is $8.50, and January 2028 forward P/E is 30, the stock price will be $260, or a two-year CAGR of 23%. I think setting a take-profit for Nvidia of $250 is wise. Beyond that point, the annual return diminishes, and contraction/volatility risk rears its head. Nvidia is a high-growth ballast right now, not a world-class 30–40% compounder anymore. It's still worth owning, but not forever. 1.5 years from today and exiting cleanly with respect for the company's ongoing growth with ~20% stock returns in a bull case is the best an elite allocator can do. If you prefer the long course, just recognize the diminishing returns on the horizon.

Risk Assessment

There are two main risks with Nvidia stock I've already outlined in this thesis:

  1. Hawkish U.S. policy as China is doubling down on undemocratic allegiances.
  2. Valuation multiple compression as the AI infrastructure growth trajectory naturally matures.

However, there are also other risks that deserve some attention here. We are all very aware of the macro and thematic bull case for Nvidia, but the risks are more subtle and nuanced (beyond the valuation, growth moderation, and China revenue constraints).

As of Q2 2026, Nvidia's largest customer comprised 23% of its total revenue, with its second largest customer at 16%. This comes at a time when hyperscalers are also developing in-house chips to reduce dependency on Nvidia. Even more broadly, the decision to significantly reduce AI capex by a major customer, like Meta Platforms (META), could cause Nvidia's valuation to bleed substantially overnight.

There's also the looming power constraints that could throttle customer deployments. If a campus can't secure megawatts and cooling, racks will be sitting idle, and orders would most likely slip for Nvidia.

These are the main risks investors have to content with, and in the next 1.5 years, I see none of them being severe enough to reduce the world-class return potential. I'm staying bullish on and invested in Nvidia, for now.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

Nvidia was 15% of my portfolio for a while, but it's now at about 8%. I feel comfortable holding on a while longer, but I do recognize that there are limitations here to long-term recurring alpha. Maybe it will be just 1.5 years before alpha diminishes, or maybe it will be 5—the trick is not to take your eye off the ball. With a company like Nvidia, the investment should be analyzed thematically and with macro awareness, as it will be the AI infrastructure capex cycle that determines Nvidia's AI-led valuation longevity. Of course, there are new verticals like robotics Nvidia can shift its dominant focus into, but I wouldn't bank on that being as aggressive a compounding trajectory without any meaningful cyclical downturn in the interim. Still, for now, Nvidia is a Strong Buy, and I'm holding on for the ride.

data.title: QYLG: Buy-Write Nasdaq-100 Strategy With A High Return Of Capital
data.description:
The Global X Nasdaq 100® Covered Call & Growth ETF (NASDAQ:QYLG) is an exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with income through a covered call strategy. QYLG is a low-cost strategy with an expense ratio of 35 bps and a distribution rate of $7.13/share, yielding 25.21% on a trailing 12-month basis, making this an appealing strategy for investors seeking income on the Nasdaq-100.

About Global X Nasdaq 100® Covered Call & Growth ETF

QYLG was launched by Global X ETFs on Sept. 18, 2020, on the Nasdaq exchange. The strategy has a relatively low expense ratio of 35 bps, offering investors a low-cost, income-oriented strategy with exposure to the Nasdaq-100. The strategy has roughly $110 million in assets under management (AUM) with an average of $857k worth of shares changing hands daily.

QYLG was designed to provide investors with both income and growth through the combination of covered call writing to generate income while holding constituents of the Nasdaq-100 for growth exposure, oftentimes called buy-write. The strategy has a total of 102 holdings. The strategy provides investors with 100% exposure to the Nasdaq-100 constituents while providing buy-write exposure to generate income on the portfolio. The strategy is indexed to the Cboe NASDAQ-100 Buy-Write Index (BXN) and is considered a passive investment vehicle.

The fund will sell 1-month covered calls on the strategy every month and roll forward the position to the next month before expiration. The fund generally writes covered calls out-of-the-money, providing room for the underlying equities to realize growth before reaching the strike price. As part of the strategy, the fund will earn a premium upon selling covered calls, providing investors with income on a monthly basis.

In addition to providing income, the covered call strategy can also provide some cushion to the fund during periods of market decline. Under this presumption, the strategy may outperform the underlying index during periods of flat or declining market activity. Given the passive nature of the fund, investors should not expect QYLG to deviate from its intended investment directive or take a defensive position during market stress.

Compared to the Nasdaq-100 (NDX), QYLG has historically underperformed given the capped growth resulting from the buy-write strategy. In general, writing covered calls on the index can cap performance depending on the growth trajectory of the underlying index between monthly call-writing periods. This can potentially create some dispersion between QYLG and NDX over a longer holding period.

QYLG Distributions

QYLG has a robust monthly distribution rate annualized at $7.13/share, yielding 25.21% on a trailing 12-month basis. Sources of distributions can vary from period to period, but historically, distributions have primarily been derived from a return on capital, making this strategy only applicable for taxable accounts. Return on capital is tax-deferred, lowering the cost basis of the investor’s position. Given this feature and the high distribution rate, QYLG can be best used for estate planning, given the cost basis reset when assets are passed along.

Given the general lag behind the underlying index, QYLG wouldn’t be appropriate for investors seeking growth or short-term capital gains on the index; investing directly in the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) may be more appropriate.

Compared to peer strategies, QYLG has one of the lower expense ratios at 35 bps, below peer strategies by ProShares (QQQA) and First Trust (FTQI). QYLG has generally outperformed peer buy-write strategies, though some of the strategies listed in the comparison are tied to the S&P 500 (SPX) and may not provide a 1:1 comparison.

Risks Related to QYLG

QYLG is a passively managed strategy indexed to the Cboe NASDAQ-100 BuyWrite Index, meaning the fund isn’t designed to beat the underlying index. QYLG will generally underperform the Nasdaq-100 Index over time, given the capped growth factor resulting from the buy-write strategy. Investors should also be aware of the tax implications when investing in the strategy; given the high return on capital, this strategy is only appropriate for taxable accounts. Selling out of the fund may result in a substantial capital gains burden that may not be ideal for most investors; the strategy may be best utilized for estate planning purposes.

Final Thoughts

QYLG is an appealing investment strategy for those seeking high returns on capital distributions, providing a 25.21% yield on a TTM basis. The strategy should primarily be used for estate planning purposes. I am recommending QYLG with a "Buy" rating with a target allocation of 4%-8%.

data.title: Lululemon Q2: Don't Be Fooled, Still Not Cheap
data.description:
Thesis Summary

On the surface, Lululemon Athletica (NASDAQ:LULU) delivered a solid Q2. EPS came in at $3.10, beating estimates by nearly 9%.

But beneath that headline number, the quarter was a disaster.

Revenue missed, guidance was slashed across the board, margins are compressing, and management openly admitted the U.S. business is faltering.

Shares dropped as much as 15% on the results, and with good reason.

Both the business and the company as an investment have lost their appeal.

Has the worst been priced in? Could this be a good chance to buy?

Let’s dig in.

Disaster Earnings

Lululemon’s Q2 2025 results were mixed at best.

EPS came in above estimates, but actually fell 1.6% YoY. Revenue was $2.525 billion, showing 6.5% growth.

However, US sales fell YoY by 0.5%.

This was made up by some growth outside of the US, especially in China.

But the bigger problem was guidance.

Q3 EPS was guided to $2.18–$2.23, a full 25% below Wall Street expectations of $2.94.

Revenue guidance for Q3 also fell short by around 3%. For the full year, EPS was slashed from $14.58–$14.78 to just $12.77–$12.97, well below the analyst consensus prior to earnings.

In other words, Lululemon management cut its earnings outlook by more than a full year of growth.

Earnings Call Insights

Management didn’t sugarcoat the problems. CEO Calvin McDonald was at least forthcoming with the issues that Lululemon is facing.

We have let our product life cycles run too long within many of our core categories… we have become too predictable within our casual offerings and missed opportunities to create new trends.

Source: Earnings Call

That casual and lounge segment, which represents about 40% of the product mix, is where weakness is concentrated.

Performance apparel (yoga, run, train, golf, tennis) is still growing, but it isn’t enough to offset the slowdown in casual.

The overall market for premium athletic wear in the U.S. remains challenging with declines continuing in quarter 2. Consumers are spending less on apparel overall… seeking out truly new styles.”

Source: Earnings Call

Looks like Lululemon is no longer the shiny new thing, with consumers moving on.

On top of that, tariffs could be a big burden on the company, as pointed out by the CFO.

We now expect a $320 million net impact on operating margin related to both higher tariffs and the removal of de minimis.

Source: Earnings Call

That’s a massive headwind that will linger into 2026. Pricing actions and supply chain adjustments can offset part of it, but not all.

The only silver lining is that China's revenues grew +25%, and the Rest of World was up +19%. But even here, management cautioned that Tier 1 Chinese cities are softening, hinting at early signs of macro fatigue.

Future Outlook

Lululemon is essentially telling investors to wait until 2026 for a real turnaround. That’s when new product initiatives could re-ignite growth.

Some areas of focus for our new design team include maintaining momentum in performance activities, designing into several new products across lounge and social, and giving a fresh perspective to some of our most iconic items.

Source: Earnings Call

The company needs to spice things up, but it's going to take some time, and the outlook for revenue and earnings is bad, especially once we take into account the effect of tariffs.

Analysts believe EPS will return to growth in 2027, but this could prove to be too optimistic. US revenue has officially begun to fall, and even management tempered down expectations on the China revenues.

Where will growth come from?

Valuation

With the stock down over 60% since its all-time high, you may think this is a good time to buy, but I would caution against it.

Even though the valuation is more enticing now, LULU still trades pretty close to the Sector Median.

Most importantly, if we look at the forward PEG, it’s actually not that cheap, with a ratio above 1.6.

We have to price in the fact that growth is slowing and that margins could get squeezed further.

Final Thoughts

I could give Lululemon a pass if I saw conviction in management, but based on the earnings call, there’s just not much to be excited about. Fashion goes in cycles, and Lulu’s popularity may have peaked.

Competition in this sector is fierce, and now the whole logistics side is at risk because of tariffs.

It may not go down much further, but I don’t see a compelling recovery right now.

data.title: Netstreit: A Retail REIT That Keeps Growing, Standing Up To Macro Headwinds
data.description:
The Stock: A Texas-Based Retail REIT With Nationwide Penetration

Although I write about a diverse array of industries, this season I'll be increasing my focus on REITs, not just because they are the theme of a book I am writing but because I think that often they get underreported in large financial media.

With that said, not every REIT is equal, and today's niche I'm talking about is the retail REIT, a good example of one being Netstreit Corp (NYSE:NTST), who is up nearly +9% since I suggested holding on to it in my May article.

From its business profile, we can learn it is a Dallas-based REIT that "specializes in acquiring single-tenant net lease retail properties nationwide."

An example of the types of properties it owns can be found in its June 2025 investor presentation:

What I liked about this stock in my spring rating was its portfolio growth and geographic diversification, while I was hesitant about its profit margins. In fact, the firm's CEO "highlighted the company’s strategy of diversifying its portfolio, a move that received a positive response from investors," in an interview last December for the Natl Assoc. of REITs.

This followup article will explore some new data since the firm's latest earnings results, and discuss where I think the niche of retail REITs may be going.

The Thesis: The Time To Bet on Quality Retail REITs Is Now, For Future Growth

My thesis today argues that my prior hold rating should be upgraded to a buy, agreeing with today's across-the-board bullish rating consensus on Seeking Alpha, and here is why:

Despite macro headwinds in the retail space for various reasons, this REIT is well diversified across different retail categories and different US geographies, as well as showing continued portfolio growth, strong margins and FFO growth, and an attractive balance sheet risk, in addition to my expecting an additional +8% upside to the most recent closing share price.

The following rating worksheet helps visualize in a structured way how I came to this rating.

The Sector: Retail May Face Some Pain Points in 2025, Yet Signs of Resiliency Are Still There

We cannot consider this REIT without understanding the niche it is in. According to that Natl Assoc. of REITs, retail REITs can be described as:

Retail REITs own and manage retail real estate and rent space in those properties to tenants. Retail REITs include REITs that focus on large regional malls, outlet centers, grocery-anchored shopping centers and power centers that feature big box retailers.

So, unlike healthcare REITs whose tenants primarily are medical practices and or senior-care facility operators, or residential REITs who own apartment complexes, what comes to mind with this niche and its underlying tenants is whether there is population growth and consumer confidence to spend money in those stores, in regions where Netstreit owns property.

A key piece of info in this niche is what financial firm UBS said in its note this June after the ICSC 2025 Conference:

We think the key theme from ICSC 2025 is that the Retail Real Estate sector is looking to power through recent uncertainty related to tariffs and consumer health. Activity levels at the conference were elevated, driven by increased volumes of leasing and transaction conversations. Industry participants remain enthusiastic given the group has bounced back from COVID and navigated several significant bankruptcies while improving portfolio quality.

I believe such a takeaway, and I know it is a lot to digest, points to added confidence in the face of this year's macro headwinds, and that should be considered an upside factor.

However, from a future view, there is also some area of concern in the retail sector.

For instance, Reuters reported on Sept 3rd that "holiday spending by U.S. consumers is set for its steepest drop since the pandemic as shoppers — particularly Gen Z — pull back amid economic uncertainty, a PricewaterhouseCoopers survey showed."

Visually, this is what the survey results look like:

So, if this expected pullback (which is a forecast at this point based on a sample survey group) comes true, or perhaps ends up being worse than projected, retailers who depend heavily on holiday sales could suffer, and I think investors may get concerned about retail REITs.

Earlier I spoke about this firm's portfolio diversification, so what I think could mitigate the macro risks of the retail sector for this REIT is that it has several tenants that are large grocery/pharmacy chains, including CVS (CVS) and Walmart (WMT), and the fact that consumers do not just buy groceries or refill their prescriptions only during holidays means that such tenants may be less prone to a holiday pullback then a clothing or electronics store might.

Adding to that is that expected fwd revenue growth is nearly +17% for Netstreit, as opposed to just +3.5% for its sector average, so I think that also sets this REIT apart in the face of potential headwinds in the retail niche.

So, I can't help but be at least neutral here, if not slightly bullish, when it comes to the sector/industry impact on this stock.

Revenue Growth: Net Growth Of Portfolio, And Potential Tailwind From Population Growth

If we look past the macro environment, let's consider company-specific growth factors that could impact the top-line in the future.

From its Q2 commentary (pg 4), Netstreit expects to what seems like continual portfolio expansion, "increasing net investment activity guidance to $125.0 million to $175.0 million from $75.0 million to $125.0 million."

This comes on top of +21 net new investments (57 investments - 36 dispositions) in the first half of this year, according to the same quarterly results (pg 2):

As I've said about REITs before, portfolio expansion can lead to future rental income flows from those properties (assuming they all get leased) and so I would said the above data is an upside factor for Netstreit and its future revenue growth potential. Consider that commercial leases are usually for many years, so new leases signed today can drive future rental income, and new properties can lead to future leases.

I also pointed out both the geographic and tenant diversification of this REIT, which I think lessens the future downside risk of over-exposure to one state or one tenant.

The portfolio is also very active in what some may call population growth areas of the US such as the southeast/southwest including states like Texas and Florida, according to the latest investor presentation:

More population growth in those regions in the future could lead to more spending in those retail establishments, which is good news for this retail REIT.

According to a January story in Newsweek, on population growth forecasts for this REIT's home state of Texas:

Texas is due to have the third highest growth rate of any American state this decade, with its population reaching an estimated 32,463,602 in 2030, according to the latest projection from the University of Virginia's Weldon Cooper Center for Public Service.

Of course, as a former resident for several years of the Texas capital, Austin, I saw that continual population growth in person because I was part of it. Despite the side effects of potential higher rents due to demand spikes, increased traffic and pressure on public infrastructure including water/sewage systems, I think it is a positive for a retail REIT who invests into that future growth, providing the retail infrastructure needed to meet the future demand.

After all, I'm not waiting until the holidays to go to the local Walmart or CVS in Austin to buy milk and juice, but probably later that day after work, along with hundreds of other people.

With that said, there are other retail REITs that investors may be considering, so I picked 4 to compare expected future revenue growth against, and they are a sampling of retail REITS including Getty Realty (GTY), Saul Centers (BFS), FrontView REIT (FVR), and Realty Income (O).

Incidentally, I happen to own a very small, nominal amount of shares in FrontView, to get a taste of this niche myself, rather than just writing about it.

In this peer comparison, Netstreit comes out ahead with a fwd revenue growth of +16.7%, the highest in the group:

Based on the above evidence, it seems the top-line growth picture for Netstreit is rosy, and worthy of bullishness.

Profit margins: An Impressive EBITDA Margin When Compared To Other Retail REITs

I know the prior few sections were a mouthful, but I think they set the stage nicely for leading into our next topic which is somewhat shorter: profit margins.

When it comes to a REIT, I care about the EBITDA margin first because it tells me about core business profitability before depreciation expense. Net income margin, which includes depreciation expense, also matters since it can impact reported profits and that influences headlines.

From the peer data, we can see that Netstreit achieved an EBITDA margin of nearly +79%, but two other REITs beat it there. Its net margin was negative at -3%, and the only other negative one in this group was its Dallas peer FrontView:

Compared to the overall sector, though, which may include many more REITs than these, profitability data shows that Netstreit achieved an above-average EBITDA margin and that is impressive.

If you are new to REITs as an asset class, I would not immediately get spooked by the negative profit margin, since often I have seen a REIT reporting net losses yet having positive cashflow growth. So, in the next section we will cover the topic of cashflow as it is important to a REIT.

Since property expenses are a major cost to any property owner, I want to point out the following from income statement data and it shows that on a YoY basis this REIT has achieved a much higher growth in revenue than growth in property expenses, which I think is a positive trend to consider:

Given the above evidence, I am slightly on the bullish side when it comes to earnings growth potential, however the analyst consensus is calling for an EPS decline this year so that is where this REIT loses some points in my rating. By comparison, Getty Realty, who also focuses on single-tenant retail properties, has a consensus calling for EPS growth this year.

Cashflow Trends: Core Operating Cash Expected To Keep Growing, As it Has Done

In this section, we will talk about cashflow trends, and cashflow matters also because dividends are paid from cash.

Notable to mention right off the bat is that not only did this firm grow its operating cashflow but also its forward operating cashflow is expected to grow +18.3%, far above its sector average:

We can also visualize growth in the cashflow statement, and even in quarters with a reported net loss this REIT added back considerable depreciation to achieve positive operating cashflow, and also a YoY growth in this number:

Both of these are upside factors, I think, and can give extra confidence to those investors who put more weight on cashflow trends than on earnings alone.

Because this is a REIT, the metric called FFO (funds from operations) is also critical and that also saw YoY growth, which can be seen in the income statement:

Besides FFO growth, this REIT has achieved dividend growth, however only has dividend history since 2020 so its 3-year dividend CAGR is +1.8%, just below the sector average growth.

Although there is a negative EPS growth forecast for this year, from the consensus, I think FFO is a better indicator in this case. According to the Corporate Finance Institute, for instance, "real estate companies use FFO as a more accurate operating performance benchmark."

Nevertheless, negative EPS outlooks can impact headlines, and investor sentiment, so poses a downside, but in terms of fundamental business survival and dividend payouts, FFO growth is key and this REIT has proven it can do so.

Balance Sheet: A Low D/E Ratio, And Major Bank Upgrade Recently

It certainly is worth considering some balance sheet metrics, especially since many REITs often use debt financing/leverage to help grow their real estate portfolio.

When comparing debt-to-equity (D/E), here is how Netstreit fares against peers:

The positive from the above data is that Netstreit has a D/E of just 0.72, the lowest in this peer group, and therefore a much lower debt in relation to equity.

This of course is something I consider an upside factor.

Further, on the risk front, I think the risk picture lowered a bit further after a major bank upgraded this stock in July. Seeking Alpha reported on July 28th that "Bank of America Securities upgraded Netstreit's rating to Neutral from Underperform after the triple net lease REIT turned in strong Q2 results."

In addition, from Netstreit's own words, in their recent investor presentation they boasted of "abundant liquidity" and also no significant debt maturities until 2028:

So, given the combined factors above, I am inclined to be on the bullish side when it comes to this firm's balance sheet risk profile.

Valuation: Just Enough Market Confidence To Make Sense

When it comes to valuation, this being a REIT, I think a good forward-looking valuation metric is the fwd P/FFO of 14.31, which is just slightly above the sector average of 13.63, so they are almost in line with each other.

This tells me the market is expecting future FFO (funds from operations) to perform close to the sector performance.

That confidence certainly can be reassuring to investors, but is it a justified valuation?

I think so, considering that this REIT has shown strong FFO growth and EBITDA margins, and continues to grow its portfolio. So, being valued just above the sector average seems to make sense, and for this reason I am adding points to the overall rating, as a reasonably valued stock.

Future Price Target: Modest +8% Upside Expected

Using the prior valuation metric, and the consensus FFO estimate, I came up with my own future price target for this stock.

I then averaged my target with the avg target shown in Seeking Alpha and Wall St Journal, and here is the table of results:

My avg price target being $19.06 for Dec. 2025, that expects a +8% upside, which is modest at best I think, but certainly does not call for selling the stock now.

To visualize potential capital gains, if you consider the price chart below I made, a future price target of $19.06 is well above todays 200-day SMA, yet there is so much more room to grow to get back to the 5-year high reached in 2021:

Challenge To My Rating: Future Tenant Financial Problems

A challenge in the future that can impact my bullish call could come if one of this REIT's top few tenants run into highly publicized financial difficulties, putting in to question their ability to keep paying rent.

From the investor presentation, we can focus on the top 3 tenants by annualized base rent, and they are Dollar General (DG), CVS (CVS) and The Home Depot (HD).

Dollar General being their top tenant, would be the one I would be most concerned about getting into trouble, as they contribute 7.5% to ABR.

The good news for now is that based on their recent Q2 results, Dollar General saw impressive performance, with "net sales increased 5.1% to $10.7B," and with the CEO saying he is "confident in the future of this resilient business model."

So, I think such great results for a key tenant can mitigate some of this risk.

Conclusion: Another Texas Success Story, Hidden in Plain Sight

To wrap up, today I presented evidence supporting why I am upgrading this REIT to a buy after my prior hold rating, and the key drivers of upside include portfolio growth in the face of retail headwinds, margin performance, FFO growth, and a low debt in relation to equity.

In a sentence, my call to action for investors would be to consider adding this Dallas-based retail REIT to your watch list of stocks to research further, as it has shown some fundamental qualities and potential future value to be unlocked, and I think the value add here is being able to fill the need for specialized retail space in those population growth areas of the US over the next few decades, which requires a specialized expertise and close relationships with key retailers looking for space.

data.title: Torrid Holdings Has Come Back To More Sensitive Valuations But Is Too Risky
data.description:
Torrid Holdings Inc. (NYSE:CURV) reported a very negative second quarter, with comps down 7%, worsening from Q1'25.

Margins are also falling significantly, and the company carries a lot of debt. In this context, the company provided guidance for breakeven net income for the fiscal year, based on adjusted EBITDA guidance.

I believe Torrid faces significant risks going forward, related to profitability and financing. The name does not qualify for investment, and I maintain a Hold rating, despite it being down more than 65% since I last wrote about it.

Q2'25 results

Sales trend worsening: The company posted comp sales down 7%. The result was blamed on footwear restructuring and the fact that last year, a marketing campaign (the model search) occurred in Q2 but will not impact Q3 more.

However, when pairing this with comps down 3.5% in Q1 and down almost 1% in Q4, I believe it is part of a wider trend of problems. In fact, comps were down in Q2'24, so it was not a difficult quarter.

Gross margins down: Gross margins decreased 300 bps, in line with Q1. In this case, the company has returned to clearance-level margins (Q2'24 had seen 300 bps improvement from lower clearance). Torrid is becoming more promotional in an effort to curtail sales losses.

The fact that the sales decrease was coupled with more promotions is worrying. Generally, you can have one or the other, but both together imply significant challenges to generate demand.

One factor adding to the gross margin decay is that lease expenses are recorded in CoGS. Therefore, not all of the impact comes from merchandise margin, but it is still the most important factor.

Tariffs incoming: The company said that the yearly tariff impact will be $50 million (almost 10% of CoGS), but that it has already offset $40 million via pricing and sourcing. This is strange, given that gross margins are falling. The company can either raise prices or lose margins to markdowns; both cannot happen together. If prices are raised but then marked down, then the net effect is a price discount, which, coupled with higher CoGS, implies even lower margins forward.

SG&A hard to shrink even with store closures: The company's SG&A was down $6 million YoY and leveraged about 20/30 bps YoY.

However, we need to consider two things. First, most of the company's sales are digital (70% referenced in the call), implying that selling expenses decrease more linearly with sales (for example, via shipping). Second, the company has closed 10% of its stores based on YTD (and has announced that it will close as many as 25% in FY25). Store closures should lead to lower labor expenses, but even in adjusted terms, SG&A margins are not improving.

Difficult to understand capital allocation: The company repurchased $20 million in stock in the quarter, leveraging its balance sheet by losing half its cash balances ($48 million in Q4'24 and now $21 million) while it adds debt ($8 million in its credit facility).

De minimis tailwind

As de minimis exemptions were removed, Torrid may, in fact, benefit from demand shifting from platforms like Shein or Temu into its own platforms. In the space of bigger sizes, having a wide assortment is a big advantage, and Shein's and Temu's massive assortment model attacked Torrid's competitive advantage. The company, which tends to index to lower-middle-income households (comments on the call about pricing being a problem, opening cheaper price points, etc., and of a customer in the $95/100 thousand yearly income range), could benefit from this.

Too risky for purchase

Torrid is guiding for sales of about $1.02 billion, or about 8% lower than last year. That is, the decrease will worsen from here on. Comments on margin were not provided, although most retailers are guiding for a worse H2'25 than H1'25 in terms of CoGS.

The company guided for adjusted EBITDA of $80/90 million, from which we need to remove $32 million in interest costs (annualized from $8 million in Q2'25), SBC of $6 million (annualized from ~$3 million in H1'25), and D&A of $40 million (equivalent to lease payments for that figure, so a real expense despite CAPEX at 25% of that level). If we add the above, we reach basically breakeven profitability, assuming no worsening of the macro outlook.

At $1.85 per share, the name trades at a market cap of $195 million. Indeed, this seems very cheap if one compares it with net income in the $20–50 million range in the pre-pandemic period. However, today the situation is very different. The company has been unable to reach pre-pandemic profitability since FY22, and it has also added a lot of debt, moving from zero interest in FY19 to $30/35 million today.

Further, the company's financial position is not risky at the time being ($20 million in cash and about $100 million in liquidity available), but it would not be able to sustain larger losses for a prolonged period (say, 2 years).

In my opinion, Torrid Holdings Inc. stock is simply too risky, with a worsening outlook, and without considering a worsening macro.

I believe the name is still a Hold, despite lower prices, unless one wants to take a very speculative position on a comeback.

data.title: Timing Is Everything With Jackson Financial Preferred Stock
data.description:
Co-authored by Cash Flow Captain

The purpose of this article is to introduce our readers to our investment logic when it comes to securities that are priced for redemption. We will focus on Jackson Financial Inc Dep Shares Reset Rate Series A Preferred Stock (NYSE:JXN.PR.A)

Company Overview

Jackson Financial Inc. (Jackson®) is committed to helping Americans grow and protect their retirement savings and income to enable them to pursue financial freedom for life. We are uniquely positioned in our markets because of our differentiated products and our well-known brand among distributors and advisors. Our market leadership is supported by our efficient and scalable operating platform and industry-leading distribution network. We believe these core strengths will enable us to grow profitably as an aging U.S. population transitions into retirement.

As of the writing of this article, (NYSE:JXN) has $6.88 billion market cap and a total debt of $5.92 billion.

Seeking Alpha assigns an A- Valuation grade. Please see key metrics below:

Jackson Financial Inc.'s IPO was on September 20, 2021. Below, we have posted a 3-year total return comparison with its peers. For this period, JXN outperforms them substantially with a total return of 265%. SA quant rating for JXN is BUY.

The company has a forward dividend yield of 3.29%. The company's most recent quarterly dividend is $0.80 per share, which amounts to approximately $223 million annually, compared to the preferred stock dividend, which is $42 million annually, so there is a significant buffer for the dividend of JXN.PR.A. from the common stock dividend.

JXN's recent earnings beat Street expectations.

The Variable Anuity Business bolsters the strong results. The variable Annuity Account is worth $239 billion.

Moody's warns about JXN's concentration of VA's with guaranteed benefits during its last periodic review:

These strengths are tempered by Jackson's concentration of VA's with guaranteed benefits, the significant exposure to equity-sensitive liabilities and asset-liability management (ALM) risks, and the limited visibility into hedging and capital management at Brooke Life Reinsurance Company.

Variable Annuities are retirement products that often track market indices and mutual funds. To attract customers, insurance companies offer guaranteed benefits. Those guarantees shift a portion of the underlying risk to the insurer, as they need to pay even if the investments underperform. In a prolonged market downturn, this puts a strain on the company's financial health. The company can hedge itself to ride through market volatility, and JXN's hedging strategy was tested during the last few volatility spikes, and it held up. However, behaviour risk is hard to hedge and predict. If policyholders withdraw guarantees during times of stress, it may cause the guarantees to exceed the account's value.

All three major credit agencies rate Jackson Financial Inc. as Stable.

The preferred stock

As of the time of writing this article, (JXN.PR.A) has a YTC of 7.08%. At the call date, its dividend will reset to 5-year US TNX + a spread of 3.73%. The market consensus for the 5-year rate on March 30, 2028, is 4.30%. The dividend after the reset should be 8.03% which is not substantially different than the current fixed dividend of 8.00%. The security is rated as follows:

  • S&P BB+
  • Moody's Ba2
  • Fitch BB+

With JXN.PR.A being priced by market participants as a probable redemption, we will take its duration of 2.87 years and assume the security will be redeemed on the first day the company can exercise the call option.

JXN's debt trades OTC. The one maturing in 2028 has a YTM of 4.466%. However, it is investment grade while JNX.PR.A is not.

OTC debt of companies rated Ba2 from Moody's, with similar duration and from the same industry, has a median YTM of 5.42%

The median yield is almost precisely the same rate as the BB US option adjusted spread (1.79%)+ 3-year US treasuries (3.627%) or 5.41%

Of course, we cannot state that maturity and priced for redemption are the same, thus we shall seek a risk premium to the median yield discussed above. We are buyers above 6.90% YTC. Below 5.68 % we sell. Please see the graph below:

Conclusion

In current market conditions, which include low credit spreads and the robust performance of JXN, with the dividend payment date approaching, we are buyers of JXN.PR.A below $26.10.

data.title: Trump Was Right: August Jobs Show Powell Is 'Too Late'
data.description:
NEW YORK (September 5) - The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) disappointed with just 22,000 new jobs for August, according to the Establishment Survey, a collection of job creation data from businesses replying to the survey. The number was well below the consensus estimate of 75,000. The private payroll number was 38,000, after taking account of 16,000 government jobs lost. For the three months of the summer (June, July, and August), average jobs was just 29,000 for the three months, the lowest since the pandemic.

The BLS’s Household Survey, which polls the number of people taking jobs, and is viewed as eliminating workers holding more than one job, printed well above the Establishment Survey at 288,000 new jobs.

The unemployment rate and the U6 rate both ticked up to 4.3% from 4.2% and 8.1% from 7.9%, respectively. The “U6” is the percentage of the population that is unemployed, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force, plus total employed part time for economic reasons, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force. Some 436,000 people joined the labor force, pushing the Labor Force Participation Rate up slightly, to 62.3% from 62.2% in July.

Let’s look at our exclusive schedule of July and August Jobs Creation by Average Weekly Wages:

Jobs creation was led, once again, by what we call “government-adjacent” jobs: Private Education & Health Services and Healthcare and Social Services, which tend to be funded by governmental programs, followed by Leisure and Hospitality. We do not include “official” government jobs payrolls in our chart, but government lost 16,000 jobs.

ECONOMY GENERALLY

Today’s numbers with last month’s massive revisions have resuscitated our recession concerns that had troubled us going back to January. We had all but put them to rest based on the jobs reports that now seem to have been illusory.

As we explained back in January, historically, a recession is almost assured after the 3-month/10-year inverted yield curve is resolved. That had happened at the end of 2024.

The data of late does show the economy is slowing significantly. First quarter GDP printed basically down at -0.5% in the third and final estimate. So called “Real GDP” - GDP minus elements of “noise,” and formally referred to as “Real final sales to private domestic purchasers" - printed at 1.9% in the revised estimate. For 2025Q2, GDP printed at 3.3% in the Second Estimate released Augusts 28th. “Real final sales to private domestic purchasers,” printed at just 1.9%.

We have thought for some time that the threat to the economy from the tariffs is considerably overblown and largely driven by media hand-wringing. We continue to believe that seller margins, not revenue, will be affected by President Trump’s tariffs as we explained in this article from last November. As we explained just recently here, the main drivers of inflation are coming from services; not goods subject to tariffs and the decline in the US Dollar Index (DXY).

Nevertheless, we continue to believe the ongoing collapse of China’s manufacturing economy we discussed here will cause China’s prices for manufactured goods to fall so that the cost of Chinese goods, after tariffs, will be within a couple of standard deviations of the 2024 average prices to American consumers. Walmart (WMT) and, we expect a number of other large U.S. importers, will use the buyers market to force their wholesale price from Chinese manufacturers down considerably.

The public seems to think inflation expectations are overblown, with the 5 year inflation expectation basically at or around 2.32, about where it was when Trump was sworn in, but up around 30 bps from where it was in April, when the President's tariff policy was first announced.

It’s clear the Fed has botched rates for at least the last two meetings. There probably should have been quarter point cuts in both meetings, given the resolution of the 3 month/10 year inverted yield curve we discussed in January as an indicator of a recession. But the Fed may be forgiven given the garbage in the BLS jobs data for the past few months. Regular followers will know that we had allayed our concerns about recession back in January, given the apparently "great" jobs numbers that were later downgraded.

Perhaps the Fed will recognized the new circumstances and be pragmatic and cut rates 50 bps in this month’s meeting, but that’s probably unlikely from a political and PR perspective. The Fed wishes to maintain the aura of Fed “independence” against a president who has been highly critical of the Fed's rate targets. We're a little confused, though, by Beveridge Curve data that show job openings and unemployment closely aligned. We may do another piece on that anomaly next week as we fear it may cause the Fed to let rates "stand pat," given ongoing Fed speculation that "tariffs will cause inflation," a view we believe to be misplaced.

PROGNOSTICATIONS

The data point to a slowing economy. However, as we said in our Q2 2025 GDP report, we think there may be substantially increased capex, an element of the Gross Domestic Investment (GDI) category of GDP, owing to the Big Beautiful Bill. However, that won’t be reflected, likely, until at least the later part of September and will not be reflected in Q3 2025 GDP.

A bigger bump in GDI will come in Q4 2025 and in 2026. In view of today’s release, we are reducing our estimate of Q3 2025 to just 0.75%, +/- 25 bps. We expect the Fed will lower rates by only a quarter point in its September meeting.

The Fed should probably go the full 50 bps cut, in our view. The economy is worse off now than it was in September of last year when the Fed cut rates 50 bps. The GDP data for 2025Q1 and 2025Q2 has been illusory, whipsawed by net exports (NEX) as companies managed their tariff expectations in Q1 with lots of imports in 2025Q1. Those declined in 2025 Q2. (A negative net exports value (a trade deficit), like we had in Q1, subtracts from GDP. A positive NEX (a trade surplus) as we had in Q1 adds to GDP.)

Optional Other Data Points

These are data points we use to assess the overall standing of the economy and are provided here for information only.

GDP printed at 3.3% in Q2 2025, according to the Second Estimate released August 28th. Core GDP, or final sales to domestic purchasers, which eliminates some of the “noise” in the GDP report, like inventories and net exports, printed at 1.9%, down from 2.7% in the Q1 2025 and 3.0% in the preliminary estimate for Q2 2024.

PCE inflation for July increased 2.6 percent. Excluding food and energy, or Core PCE inflation, which is, reportedly, the Fed’s preferred measure of inflation, increased 2.9% from one year ago according to data released August 29th.

The Institute for Supply Management's Manufacturer's Purchasing Managers Index ((PMI)) printed at 48.7 for August, up 70 bps from July. The overall manufacturing sector has been declining for 6 months, albeit slower in August.

The August services Index grew to 52.0 from July’s 50.1

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) for July printed Wednesday, with 176,000 fewer job openings than in June, but with 41,000 more hires.

Privately-owned housing units authorized by building permits in July, released August 29th, were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,354,000. This is 2.8 percent below the revised June rate of 1,393,000 and is 5.7 percent below the July 2024 rate of 1,436,000.

Personal income increased $112.3 billion (0.4 percent at a monthly rate) in July, according to estimates released August 29th by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. Disposable personal income (DPI)—personal income less personal current taxes—increased $93.9 billion (0.4 percent) and personal consumption expenditures (PCE) increased $108.9 billion (0.5 percent).

RealClearMarkets/TIPP Economic Optimism Index for September fell from 50.9 in August to 48.7, a 2.2-point decline (-4.3%).

_________________________________________

NOTE: Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/ geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe are underperforming. While we have never written about any companies that are our clients and do not include companies we write about here in our personal investment portfolios, we do provide here strategies that we would have recommended were the companies our clients. Others discuss new management strategies of non-client companies that we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change.)

data.title: BMW Plus Qualcomm Equals The Automated Driving Play
data.description:
Introduction

I enjoy the kind of news like the one we just got from Qualcomm Incorporated (NASDAQ:QCOM) and BMW (OTC:BMWKY). I already picture a hall in Munich with the new BMW iX3 there, looking like no other car, doors closed, screens on, and all of this room smells like an expensive vehicle. This car is powered by a driver-assist system that was built for three years by BMW and Qualcomm, ready to help me with any of my needs. Dreaming is nice, but I am not in Munich and it’s not 2028 yet, so time to discuss what it means for both companies and for the Autonomous Driving as a sector.

It looks like the car industry has stopped waiting for humanity to switch to robotaxis that drive themselves everywhere and realized we can take this whole thing into a completely different direction, which potentially can be even more profitable. I am referring to the Level 2 Plus, which is already available and allowed by many regulators throughout the world. It’s not full self-driving, or FSD, but it can help the driver on long rides by handling the boring stuff and updating over time to get better. As you probably realized by now, Qualcomm makes the hardware and the software, and BMW is responsible for the car and the driving part.

What was announced

Qualcomm and BMW introduced Ride Pilot, which they have been working on for the past three years, and we can actually see that it’s not a raw agreement that was just signed. The company said that “System is validated in 60 countries worldwide and is targeted to be available in more than 100 countries by 2026,” which shows how big of progress has already been made. Regulators are always one of the biggest headwinds in this sector, so knowing they already have a huge market for dealerships is great.

They called this system Snapdragon Ride Pilot. The word “pilot” is there for a reason - this thing behaves like an actual, very capable co-pilot. On certain highways that are mapped and approved, it can keep the car in the lane, decide the speed, make safe lane changes, and even park itself. But the important part is that a human is still the driver. You can take off the hands sometimes, but the idea is that your eyes stay on the road, and you stay responsible. It’s advanced assistance that makes long, boring rides easier, but doesn't deliver you on its own.

A nice part is that this system isn’t limited to one country or a couple of streets, as I already said, there are already 60 countries, with a plan to reach 100. It means the hardware and software have been checked against local rules in all of those countries, road signs, streets, and driving conditions, so the feature can legally and safely turn on where it’s allowed.

It’s debuting in the all-new iX3 at IAA Mobility, and, importantly, Qualcomm is now offering this same system to any global automaker or Tier-1 supplier that wants it, which could prove to be a new powerful growth engine both for the company and its stock. Basically, now they turn a BMW partnership into a platform play for the rest of the industry.

BMW’s part

For BMW, the Neue Klasse is basically a fresh start. It’s the new way they’re building cars. The idea is that everything runs on software, and instead of having lots of little computers around the vehicle, it will have one strong central system. That’s what makes it possible to add things like hands-free highway driving or smooth lane changes. In the iX3, BMW calls these central units “superbrains,” and the one that controls the automated driving runs on Qualcomm’s Snapdragon Ride. They say it’s about twenty times more powerful than what they used before. For the company, it’s a chance to keep innovating and creating new features that will make them interesting again. While BMW, AUDI, and Mercedes are without a doubt great brands, newer, modern automakers, often Chinese electric manufacturers, catch the attention of the European crowd with electronics and new, cool features. Legacy brands need things like this not to lose their relevance.

Now, does it mean BMW will be discussed on the market as much as Xpeng (XPEV), Nio (NIO), BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), or Tesla (TSLA)? I don’t think so. It takes more than one partnership to change the narrative, but it will be worth following this company in the future and seeing maybe they have something more to offer.

Why Qualcomm wins here

For Qualcomm, at the same time, it’s a pretty big deal. This company spent years trying to move from phones into cars, first with infotainment and connectivity, then into safety-critical compute and an ADAS stack. And now, finally, this company will be able to present something physical you can touch and sit in.

What they’re rolling out is a system that sees the road mainly through cameras, with radar built in to back it up, which is tied into a safety framework that works across different countries. The software will keep learning through data collected from cars already on the road. The focus isn’t on some distant idea of fully driverless cars with no steering wheel, but on making Level 2 Plus work reliably across different markets. That’s also where most of the demand is today. And the fact that Qualcomm is offering Ride Pilot to other automakers right at launch shows the aggressive approach the company chose. It looks like they are ready to capture the market share.

How it compares

But not everything is perfect. This is a space with a lot of players already. Nvidia (NVDA) is training a very powerful, modular system, while Mobileye has been in the game the longest and is known for dependable safety features. Tesla and General Motors (GM) are building their own versions, too. What BMW and Qualcomm are doing looks more practical because they’re focusing on a Level 2 Plus system that’s built around safety, can be approved in many markets, and can grow with software updates, but still, there is no way of knowing yet if it will win the competition. In the end, the question is who can roll out hands-free driving that regulators will sign off on, drivers will actually trust, and automakers can put in cars across their lineups. On those points, Ride Pilot is starting from a strong position, but once again, any minor setback and this system can lose the competitive advantage forever.

The things to watch are:

  1. Regional feature maps: where hands-free is approved, how quickly BMW adds roads, and how they message the supervision requirement to avoid driver complacency.
  2. Packaging and price: whether this shows up standard on higher iX3 trims or as a subscription, and how that translates to take-rates.
  3. Expansion beyond BMW: how quickly do we see a second and third automaker adopt the same system, and at what sensor tier.

Besides, it’s very important whether the system keeps improving after people start driving it. The whole idea behind pairing BMW’s Neue Klasse with Ride Pilot is that the car shouldn’t feel outdated a year later, it should get smarter as time goes on. That comes down to how often the software gets updated and how smoothly those updates work. If everything just works without problems, then it’s doing what it’s supposed to, but we will be able to see that only after a decent amount of time passes.

Conclusion

I see this as the version of automated driving that actually has a chance to spread widely in the shorter term than a full autonomous vehicle. It’s basically a set of features you can already put in Berlin, Boston, or Tel Aviv without having many problems, and the demand for it promises to be decent. People are more and more into buying cars for software and electronics rather than engine qualities. The iX3 launch gives BMW a proper reset with modern computing power, and by opening the system to other automakers, Qualcomm is turning one product launch into a whole new business line.

When you look at the Qualcomm stock, the valuation adds weight to the case. Shares trade at forward earnings multiples well below the sector average, with a forward P/E around 13–15x compared to peers closer to 23–30x. Even on an EV/EBITDA basis, Qualcomm sits at a discount, the same as compared to its own 5-year average. The growth is pretty much still there, and the future looks interesting. To me, that combination makes Qualcomm a Buy at current levels. BMW will need to do better than one partnership to win a place in my portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AT&T: How The EchoStar Deal Is Different
data.description:
AT&T (NYSE:T) made an announcement that it closed a deal with EchoStar (SATS) to essentially acquire low-band and mid-band spectrum licenses for roughly $23 billion. The overall sales point is that this will provide future growth prospects for AT&T while allowing whole business growth. EchoStar, for its part under its "Boost Mobile" brand, will continue to operate to obtain customers that are not part of the AT&T marketing plan. This is sold as a "win-win" for both companies. This is a different strategy than the last one, which was presented to shareholders as accretive from the start.

Part of the reason for this is a brief reference during the conference call that the low band was not proceeding as planned (for EchoStar), which was concerning to the FTC. A little-noticed part of the deal (for the lower bands) is that AT&T has a plan to fix the situation after the deal closes, but it may take a few years, as AT&T itself does not have much of a presence in that area of the market. It is also likely something that has to be approved by the regulatory bodies, as AT&T is buying something that would be lost through noncompliance.

The strategy for this deal is similar to the one detail in my coverage of Chart Industries (GTLS) (which is now going to be acquired by Baker Hughes (BKR)). In the 40-plus years that I covered Chart Industries, it grew by becoming a "one-stop shop" for more and more customers. The idea behind acquisitions was to accretively attract sales that the two parts could not accomplish separately. The result was the company grew rapidly through these types of acquisitions, as I documented through articles over the years.

For AT&T, first of all, the company is far larger, and secondly, this is the first transaction in this direction since the widely panned "shopping spree" that ended up largely creating the company as it now exists. This is one of those deals where the market hears the story and then goes "yah yah," followed by a yawn.

The reason is that in terms of strategy, a whole lot of companies do this, and then the market ends up asking, "where are the results?" It does not help that management stated it would take about 36 months for the transaction to become accretive. But this is a situation where the company is acquiring existing expansion capacity from someone that either does not need it or cannot do what is necessary to comfortably maintain the licenses. Therefore, this purchase does not fill up "the day after" the transaction closes. Instead, it will take some time for this to happen.

The other thing to consider is that this transaction expands an existing business with a little bit in a new area, whereas before it was a "shopping spree" of all kinds of new businesses that the company thought were needed for it to survive in the future. Here, the company is buying essentially a raw material (if you will) that it knows very well what to do with, as it is doing it in other areas of the business. As long as there is not another shopping spree of like transactions, management is unlikely to be overwhelmed with acquisition-type expenditures and growth plans that would lead to another borrowing binge.

Furthermore, there is a plan to get the debt ratio back down within roughly 36 months after the transaction closes. That is after considering that a debt ratio in the 3 range is probably nothing to be concerned about in the first place. Instead, it is more about getting that debt ratio lower so that the company is in a position to take advantage of deals as they appear.

That means this one deal is not a "company wrecker" if it fails. In fact, it probably has more than an average chance to succeed. By all means, management should be held accountable for the progress of the pending deals and then the accuracy of the "sales pitch" after the deal closes. But there is no need to panic that the company is "throwing money away" based upon the fears of what happened before.

Last Article

My last article noted that the income statement was cleaning up with the final significant divestiture of the DirecTV holdings announced. But this announcement, combined with some previous announcements, will include some nonrecurring costs when these various deals close.

These nonrecurring costs should not last nearly as long as it took to clean up earnings from all the divestitures and refocusing that took place.

The other thing is that debt is going to climb again. The difference is that one deal is supposedly accretive the day it closes, while this one will benefit the company in the form of more growth opportunities. Also, the value of the combined deals is less than $30 billion (without considering adjustments).

That combined value is far less than what happened during the "shopping spree." That means there are a lot of differences between the latest deals compared to what the market fears from the past. But it also means that management has to prove what it says are the goals. I personally like management's chances. Now let us see what happens.

Summary

AT&T seems to have a deal that has darn good chances to succeed. Management strategy books like Michael Porter's "Competitive Strategies" detail what it takes for a business combination to succeed. Forays into new businesses are obviously the riskiest (and for good reason). But this appears to be a well-thought-out expansion of offerings into areas that might have been half-served or avoided altogether.

In sum, the latest strategic announcements appear to be acquisitions that will profitably expand the business. In any event, none are so large that they are "bet the company" transactions.

Still, it may be time to lower my rating from a Strong Buy to a Buy. I still believe that the stock is a good deal. But the stock price reaction to the announcement may mean that the bargain that existed a while back is slowly disappearing. The price-earnings ratio is fine for those that want to get in, and the dividend yield is down from past highs but still reasonable. This is still a good play as a large company growth and income idea. I just think the recovery potential that existed for some time is now largely gone, and so it is time to reduce the rating. But for what the company appears to offer going forward, the current price is reasonable.

Risks

Third quarter earnings are due out on October 22. This is the first chance that management will have to report any unfavorable changes to the announced deals. Deals can come undone for any number of foreseen and unforeseen events. It is unlikely if the proper due diligence was done before the deal was announced. But it can happen.

Any acquisition can fail to meet future expectations. This risk is minimized in that the company is acquiring assets that it knows very well what to do with those assets once they are acquired. This is very different from acquiring new businesses.

Right now, there exists a fear of economic uncertainty. The business here is fairly recession resistant. However, a severe and sustained economic downturn could materially alter the outlook of the company.

data.title: New Gold: Still A Buy Despite The Recent Surge
data.description:
Introduction

Since my article titled New Gold: Potentially Perfect Timing Makes It A Buy was published, New Gold (NYSE:NGD) is up significantly. However, the key thing to remember here is that the stock’s performance had very little to do with the company’s recent developments, which I’ll cover below, with the stock remaining almost flat on earnings day and even a bit after it.

Why It Surged

The stock is up about 35% since I called it a Buy. As I said in the analysis, rate cuts will be a key catalyst for gold and gold miners, and we can see a majority of them up significantly since August, with the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) being up ~25.5% since the end of July. This, combined with trade deals, renewed threats, recession fears, and even seeing tariffs being ruled illegal by a federal appeals court a few days ago (which could lead to the US even having to refund $200 billion to businesses), led to what the market hates a lot: uncertainty—which is amazing for gold, as we can see (the commodity is up 8.4% since the end of July to a new all-time record).

Especially following Powell’s Jackson Hole remarks, the market sees rate cuts as something that’s significantly more likely to happen in the near future, which will have a clear effect on the economy, the stock market, gold, and more. As of a few days ago, the markets were pricing in a nearly 92% chance of a 25 basis points cut at the Fed's September 17 meeting, so we’re talking about very high expectations.

To be fair, I think this is more about getting a more tangible date and a reconfirmed focus on rate cuts rather than expecting them “in the future”—leading ”to less uncertainty around a catalyst that’s potentially very beneficial for gold prices, alongside many other commodities. Rate cuts would lead to lower treasury yields, which means a lower opportunity cost for holding gold,

At the same time, fear around an “accidental hard landing” is rising due to potentially weaker jobs data, rising consumer inflation, and a delayed effect of tariffs on prices fueled (and inflation) fueling concerns. This uncertainty is once again great for gold thanks to its status, while rate cuts can lead to lower treasury yields and a weaker USD due to money chasing better yields.

However, I’d like to reiterate that this is a general industry thing, not a company-specific one, and the potential of the initial thesis remains unchanged—or actually enhanced if gold prices remain high.

The Earnings Report

The company’s Q2 production and costs were as expected, with production of approximately 78,600 oz of gold and 13.5 million lb of copper. As they say, this increase in gold production compared to Q2’24 has been driven by the planned higher feed grade at Rainy River, partially offset by lower planned feed grade at New Afton. The AISC for the quarter was $1,393 per ounce, similar to Q2 last year and much better than Q1 2025, and what’s even better is that the company expects it to continue to go down throughout the year as production increases, which is part of their potential.

Also worth noting is that the overall gold production in H1 was ~38% of the midpoint of the consolidated production guidance of 325,000 to 365,000 oz, consistent with the planned 38% stated in the February outlook, so the company has been almost perfectly on point with this, and the reliability of their projections and their ability to execute on them going forward can lead to massive developments, as I’ll mention below.

The New Afton mine is expected to continue producing 60,000 to 70,000 ounces of gold and 50,000 to 60,000 lb of copper in 2025, which is in line with 2024. However, this was achieved at no cost while the company made some improvements for the mine, with mine life expected to be pushed for one more year (until 2031) and potential for more.

At Rainy River, we see a similar story, with lower production in H1 that will be ramped up to potentially reach an expected 265,000 to 295,000 ounces vs. ~225,700 ounces in 2024, but it’s also worth noting that Rainy River experienced operational challenges in December that led to them missing its gold production guidance for the year (between 230,000 and 240,000 ounces for this mine). However, the company is expanding the mine’s open-pit operations thanks to Phase 5, which should expand its lifetime from the initial 2028 depletion date, as well as a focus on developing its underground operations, which comes with the breakthrough of the underground ramp to the pit portal announced in April and the ventilation loop completed in late June. These are key steps that should allow the company to boost production from several new mining zones, expected to come online in 2025.

Reiterating My Thesis

Given the company’s growth potential, we’re likely looking at a free cash flow of as much as $1 billion in 2026, which is still amazing for a $4.8 billion market cap. However, I believe that currently they aren’t trading at the same valuations as most other peers because of the short life of mines, even though the company will have a significant boost of capital over the next couple of years.

As they say, 2024 and especially 2025 should be significant inflection points for the free cash flow, especially thanks to gold now reaching new all-time records. As I said in the analysis, we might be looking at potentially perfect timing from the company in many aspects.

First, we have the 19.9% free cash flow interest acquisition in New Afton, allowing the company to have full ownership now, which was done back in April, potentially adding significant cash flows going forward. On top of this, we have what they called “exceptional” drill results back in September 2024, which could boost the cash flows for the long term.

And that’s where the big risk comes into play. Yes, the company is expecting massive drops in costs and CAPEX after 2025 since they’re finishing these ramp-ups, allowing them to enjoy potentially phenomenal 2026 and 2027 numbers if gold prices remain high. However, what happens after? Sure, there’s underground potential in Rainy River and New Afton can run, especially as a copper operation in the longer term, but that’s uncertainty, which the market doesn’t like, as we know.

They’ll gain a massive amount of cash flow that’s more than enough to do very significant acquisitions and expand production through other mines for the long term, but what if they decide to spend most of that money on dividends, or the project/projects they buy turn out to be a failure, as we’ve seen happening, especially for smaller miners, before? This is what keeps me from rating them a Strong Buy and only a normal Buy even now, as the management’s decisions can make the difference between an amazing long-term potential and a mediocre one.

And relying on two mines is also a problem, as we’ve seen even back in December 2024 with the operational challenges mentioned at their Rainy River mine. This time, it was only some unexpected mechanical downtime on the crushing and conveying system, resulting in lower-than-expected throughput at the mill, but what if the next issue will be much more significant? They not only rely on two mines, but Rainy River accounts for about 77% of their production, so of course, the market takes this into account when valuing the company.

Valuation

However, I believe that the current price is still far from the company’s potential if things go okay and they manage to offset the declining mines through acquisitions. Despite gold’s surge to all-time highs, I’ll keep the valuation unchanged to be more conservative, even though I remain bullish on gold. Any jump above the initial estimate will be a very welcome bonus.

As you can see from the updated valuation that includes their latest numbers (while we keep the assumption for gold at $3,250/oz, assuming that it falls from the current record level over this time), if they reach their 2025, 2026, and 2027 targets (so far, so good based on H1 2025) and account for a massive 8% FCF decline rate from 2028 to 2030, after which they remain flat and only assume a 2% terminal growth rate with a 9% WACC, we’re still potentially looking at a good return even after the recent jump. The key factor here will be proving their potential and how they'll utilize the cash flows that should come over the following years. If they do this and announce some merger, acquisition, or some developments that can at least flatline that production from 2030 onward after the decline we assumed, this stock and the company have significant potential.

Conclusions

Overall, New Gold remains a Buy in my opinion, thanks to the massive potential they have, thanks to the cash flows that should come over the coming few years, with a good balance sheet and significant upside potential. However, the company’s reliance on two mines and uncertainty around how the management will address their declining production keep me from rating it a Strong Buy, as their future will depend significantly on the management’s decisions.
